questions.question: What makes time-series workloads different from general OLTP workloads?
options: Time-series data has frequent updates and deletes
Writes are append-heavy and arrive in near-time order; reads are range scans over time windows
Time-series databases use B-Trees optimized for random writes
Time-series data has no schema or data types
questions.explanation: Time-series workloads are append-heavy (new data always has the most recent timestamp), writes arrive in time order, and the dominant read pattern is range queries over a time window. This makes B-Trees suboptimal — time-windowed block storage with purpose-built compression is far more efficient.

questions.question: In event sourcing, how do you obtain the current state of an entity?
options: Read the latest row from a state table
Fold/replay the event stream through a deterministic apply function
Query a materialized view that is always up to date
Run an UPDATE statement on the events table
questions.explanation: Current state = fold(event_stream, initial_state, apply_fn). Every event is replayed in order through a pure function. This is fundamentally different from CRUD, where you mutate state in place and lose history.

questions.question: The Gorilla compression scheme compresses float values in a time-series by:
options: Rounding all values to integers
Storing deltas between consecutive values as variable-length integers
XORing each value with the previous value and storing only the bits that changed
Using run-length encoding on repeated values
questions.explanation: Gorilla XOR compression exploits the fact that consecutive float measurements in a time-series are close in value. XORing adjacent values produces long runs of leading and trailing zeros, which compress very efficiently (often 1-4 bits per value instead of 64).

questions.question: A time-series database stores raw 1-second sensor readings. A user queries the weekly average over the past year. Without downsampling, this query requires scanning ~31 million points. The most efficient storage-level optimization is:
options: Add a B-Tree index on the timestamp column
Pre-compute and store downsampled rollups (e.g., 1-hour buckets) and expire old raw data per retention policy
Store all data in a single file for sequential scanning
Use a hash index on the value column
questions.explanation: Downsampling creates pre-aggregated rollups at coarser time granularities (5m, 1h, 1d). A query for weekly averages over a year uses ~52 1-hour-bucket points instead of 31M raw points. Retention policies delete the raw data after it has been rolled up, keeping storage bounded.

questions.question: CQRS (Command Query Responsibility Segregation) is often paired with event sourcing. What is the primary benefit of this pairing?
options: It reduces the total storage required for events
It allows the write model (event log) and read model (materialized views) to be independently optimized for their access patterns
It eliminates the need for database indexes
It guarantees that events are always processed in parallel
questions.explanation: CQRS separates the write path (commands that produce events) from the read path (queries against pre-built views). The event log is optimized for append-only writes; the read model can use any structure (denormalized tables, aggregations, caches) for fast queries. If the read model gets stale or corrupted, you can rebuild it by replaying events — the event log is the single source of truth.

questions.question: In the time-series block storage model (e.g., Prometheus 2-hour blocks, our Block class), a block is immutable once closed. What problem does this immutability create, and how is it solved?
options: New data cannot be inserted into closed blocks — the engine must create new blocks, and old blocks are eventually compacted/merged into larger blocks via background compaction
Immutability causes write amplification because every write creates a new file — solved by switching to in-place updates
Closed blocks cannot be read — solved by keeping all data in memory
Immutability is a problem only for event sourcing, not for time-series
questions.explanation: Once a block is closed (because its time window has passed), no new data is written to it. The engine creates a new block for the current time window. Old blocks are compacted in the background — merged into larger blocks and optionally downsampled. This is exactly how Prometheus's TSDB works: 2-hour blocks are written once, then compacted into larger blocks.
